InteractionModes.Name: Cooperation
InteractionModes.Definition: Local mutual enhancement between elements, leading to stable patterns and modules through synergistic interactions.

PhaseRoles.A: Rudimentary compatibility between differentiated elements, preparation of patterns.
PhaseRoles.B: Dominant integration mechanism: assembly of stable patterns and modules.
PhaseRoles.C: Support for mode alignment in the presence of feedback.
PhaseRoles.D: At critical saturation, strengthens the threshold transition but does not define it alone.

ActivationConditions: Functional complementarity/compatible interfaces
Sufficient local density of connections
Common context/resource support, excluding competing modes
FailureModes: False synergy (hidden dependence on an external node)
Sticking in a local optimum (excessive cooperation \to loss of variability)
Creeping cooperation and blurring of node roles

Flows.Energy: Reduction of dissipation through action alignment
Flows.Information: Redundancy and increased reliability of encoding

MathForm.Notes: Positive summands in effective equations, in CRT cooperation is realized by a cubic term.

Predictions: Growth of clustering and k-core precedes the formation of stable patterns
FalsifiableIf: The rupture of a small number of key cooperative edges destroys the pattern while the parameters of the elements remain unchanged

InteractionModes.Name: Constraint
InteractionModes.Definition: Narrowing the state space due to form/context/rules, channels trajectories and fixes admissibility.

PhaseRoles.D: Filter of new organizational logic, readjustment of admissibilities.

ActivationConditions: The presence of a stable form/rule/boundary
Alignment of the scale of constraints with the scale of dynamics
Resource ceilings/norms that exclude unsuitable modes
FailureModes: Reconstraint \to fragility/failure under perturbations
Inconsistency of the scale of constraints and microdynamics
Illegitimate constraint (meta-conflict at the level of rules)

Flows.Energy: Channeling of flows and reduction of excessive degrees of freedom
Flows.Information: Increase in predictability (reduction of conditional entropy)
Flows.Matter: Formation of admissible geometry (niches, orbitals, packing templates)

MathForm.Notes: Constraints as \(\phi(x) \le 0\) with Lagrange multipliers, projections onto the admissible subspace.

predictions: Tightening K reduces the set of achievable trajectories and increases the repeatability of the effect
falsifiable_if: Loosening/removing K does not expand the behavioral repertoire and does not reduce the repeatability of the observed effect

InteractionModes.Name: Feedback
InteractionModes.Definition: Closed cycles of influence (including lateral and diagonal ones) that change the system’s mode stabilize or transfer it to another state.

PhaseRoles.B: Weak contours of stabilization of collected patterns appear.

ActivationConditions: The presence of a closed loop (cycle) in the interaction graph
Effective loop gain coefficient g (g>1 — positive, 0<g<1 — negative/stabilizing)
In the presence of delays — coordination of time scales
FailureModes: Destabilization (unbounded positive loop)
Autonomous oscillations/chaos (delays + amplification)
Overdamping (loss of adaptability)

Flows.Energy: Redistribution of power between loops, mode retention
Flows.Information: Amplification/suppression of signals, reduction of regulation error
Flows.Matter: Recycling of material flows in closed loops

MathForm.Notes: Closed system of equations with inverse transfer matrix, criticality at \(det(I-G)=0\).
Equations: \(x_{t+1} = A x_t + B y_t,  y_t = C x_t  \Rightarrow  x_{t+1} = (A + B C) x_t\)

Predictions: An increase in the spectral radius in the circuit precedes a change in the mode
The introduction of a negative loop reduces output variance
FalsifiableIf: The closure of explicit cycles does not change modes under otherwise equal conditions (excluding hidden loops)

InteractionModes.Name: Initiation
InteractionModes.Definition: Local triggering act/condition that translates latent permissiveness into active unfolding of the configuration.

PhaseRoles.B: Initiation of cooperative assembly upon reaching density.
PhaseRoles.C: Activation of regulatory loops from a latent state.
PhaseRoles.D: Triggering of a threshold transition (minimal external/internal impulse).

ActivationConditions: The presence of structural potential (the configuration already allows for transition)
Exceeding the local threshold \theta (amplitude/gradient/concentration)
Access to a resource/channel for spreading the effect
FailureModes: Insufficient amplitude (\theta not reached)
False trigger (one-off surges without supporting configuration)
Premature activation in the absence of ready contours

Flows.Matter: Nucleus/seed (nucleation) or local source of reagent

MathForm.Notes: Threshold/sigmoid activation functions, stochastic acts of escaping the pit.

Predictions: With potential, a small impulse initiates long-term unfolding
Without potential, even large impulses produce damped responses
FalsifiableIf: In the absence of potential, small impulses systematically lead to stable novelty

InteractionModes.Name: Realization
InteractionModes.Definition: Transformation of the admitted configuration of potential into an observable action/effect, fixation of a new function/quality.

PhaseRoles.A: Rarely, local micro-realizations as signals of permissiveness.
PhaseRoles.C: Functional realization at the level of contour/mode.
PhaseRoles.D: Main fixation of the effect and the birth of a new contour/level.

ActivationConditions: Fulfillment of permissive conditions (potential + activating connections)
Availability of fixation/observation channel (operationalization)
Closure of the contour (stability of the effect under small perturbations)
FailureModes: Latent potency without fixation (no channel/conditions)
Disruption of realization due to conflicting constraints
Substitution of the effect with measurement artifacts

Flows.Energy: Transition to a mode with a new power distribution

MathForm.Notes: Mapping of permissiveness to measurable effect, observed as functionals of the field/state.

Predictions: In the presence of potency + trigger, a stable effect node arises (an increase in the input degree of the «effect» node)
Fixation of the effect is accompanied by the appearance of a new closed circuit including the effect-node
FalsifiableIf: The effect is stably fixed without the presence of closed loops and with control of hidden loops

CausalDirections.Name: Upward causation
CausalDirections.Definition: Causal direction in which interactions and configurations at a lower descriptive level generate structures, properties, or dynamics at a higher level. Causality is expressed through aggregation, coarse-graining, or composition.
FormalCriteria: Macrovariables are functions of microvariables
Higher-level description is undefined without lower-level interactions
Removal of micro-interactions eliminates the macro-phenomenon

MathForm.Description: Upward causation is expressed by constructive mappings from microstates to macrostates.

DetectionTests: Altering micro-interactions changes macro-behavior
Macro-structure cannot be postulated independently
Multiple microstates map onto the same macrostate
FailureModes: Confusing correlation with generation
Ignoring necessary aggregation scale

CausalDirections.Name: Downward causation
CausalDirections.Definition: Causal direction in which higher-level structures or states constrain, modulate, or parameterize the behavior of lower-level components. The macro-level shapes admissible micro-dynamics.
FormalCriteria: Micro-dynamics include parameters or constraints derived from macrostate
Macrostate acts as a boundary condition or control variable
Micro-equations change form depending on macro-context

MathForm.Description: Downward causation appears as constraints or control parameters imposed on micro-dynamics.

DetectionTests: Changing macrostate alters micro-level trajectories
Micro-components behave differently under identical local conditions but different macro-contexts
Macro-removal restores broader microstate space
FailureModes: Misinterpreting feedback as pure downward control
Attributing global influence without explicit constraints

CausalDirections.Name: Horizontal causation
CausalDirections.Definition: Causal interactions occurring between components or processes at the same descriptive level, forming local dynamics without hierarchical asymmetry.
FormalCriteria: Interacting variables belong to the same level of description
No coarse-graining or level transition is required
Causality is symmetric or peer-based

MathForm.Description: Horizontal causation is represented by direct couplings between peer variables.

DetectionTests: Removing one peer directly affects another at the same level
Causal influence does not propagate through a higher-level variable
Interaction graph remains intra-level
FailureModes: Hidden mediation through implicit macrovariables
Collapsing collective effects into horizontal links

CausalDirections.Name: Diagonal causation
CausalDirections.Definition: Causal direction characterized by closed or intertwined influence paths that span multiple levels simultaneously, integrating upward, downward, and horizontal components into a single circuit.
FormalCriteria: Presence of feedback loops crossing levels
Mutual dependence between micro- and macro-variables
No single direction suffices to explain the dynamics

MathForm.Description: Diagonal causation appears in coupled multi-level systems with feedback.

DetectionTests: Breaking either upward or downward link destroys the phenomenon
Stability depends on cross-level loop gain
Emergent behavior arises only in the closed system
FailureModes: Reducing diagonal causation to simple feedback
Ignoring time-scale coordination between levels

CausalDirections.Name: Decoupled causation
CausalDirections.Definition: Causal direction in which interactions between higher-level entities are governed by effective parameters that abstract away micro-level details. Microstates are statistically or structurally marginalized.
FormalCriteria: Effective equations omit explicit micro-variables
Macro-dynamics remain valid across micro-variations
Existence of renormalized or averaged parameters

MathForm.Description: Decoupled causation is described via effective theories.

DetectionTests: Changing micro-details does not affect macro-behavior
Macro-predictions remain accurate without micro-resolution
Explicit micro-reconstruction is unnecessary
FailureModes: Applying decoupling outside its validity regime
Confusing ignorance with effective independence

CausalDirections.Name: Metacausation
CausalDirections.Definition: Causal direction operating at the level of causal admissibility itself, defining when and how causal relations can be formulated, compared, or validated.
FormalCriteria: Concerns rules of causation rather than causal flows
Operates on models, logics, or representational frameworks
Defines constraints on what counts as a cause

MathForm.Description: Metacausation is formalized through meta-rules and logical structures.

DetectionTests: Altering meta-rules changes admissible causal explanations
Causality fails without these conditions
No direct empirical signal without instantiating lower levels
FailureModes: Treating metacausation as physical interaction
Embedding meta-rules into object-level dynamics

TypeRoles.Name: Process
TypeRoles.Definition: Emergent phenomena understood as time-extended transformations that exist only through ordered change of system state. A process is present when the phenomenon cannot be defined without an explicit temporal parameter and requires a trajectory, flow, or evolution operator for its description.
FormalCriteria: Explicit dependence on time or ordering parameter t
Existence defined by state transition rather than static configuration
Absence of the phenomenon in frozen or time-collapsed descriptions

MathForm.Description: Processes are represented by dynamical equations defining trajectories in state space.

DetectionTests: Removal of temporal evolution eliminates the phenomenon
Different initial conditions yield distinct trajectories of the same process
Process identity is preserved across realizations with varying substrates
FailureModes: Misclassification of stable dynamics as objects
Reduction of a process to a single snapshot or outcome

TypeRoles.Name: Object
TypeRoles.Definition: Emergent phenomena understood as persistent configurations stabilized by internal coherence and admissible constraints. An object exists as a distinguishable carrier that remains identifiable under small perturbations.
FormalCriteria: Existence of a stable invariant, attractor, or bounded region in state space
Persistence under perturbations within a defined tolerance
Definable boundary separating the object from its environment

MathForm.Description: Objects correspond to invariant or weakly attracting structures in the system description.

DetectionTests: The phenomenon can be referenced as a stable unit
Removal of internal coherence destroys object identity
Object persists across multiple realizations of the same constraints
FailureModes: Confusion of transient patterns with objects
Overextension of objecthood to loosely correlated assemblies

TypeRoles.Name: Pattern
TypeRoles.Definition: Emergent phenomena understood as reproducible regularities or motifs that recur across components, time, or scales. A pattern is defined by form or relation rather than by a specific carrier.
FormalCriteria: Repetition or invariance under transformation
Independence from a single physical instance
Statistical or structural regularity across realizations

MathForm.Description: Patterns are described via symmetry, correlation, or equivalence classes.

DetectionTests: The same structure appears in multiple locations or instances
Pattern survives replacement of individual components
Statistical measures reveal non-random regularity
FailureModes: Mistaking single instances for patterns
Confusing objects with their repeated arrangements

TypeRoles.Name: Property
TypeRoles.Definition: Emergent phenomena understood as system-level attributes that cannot be assigned to individual components but characterize the system as a whole. A property is a derived macroscopic parameter.
FormalCriteria: Defined as a function of many microstates
Irreducible to any single component variable
Well-defined only at a specific descriptive scale

MathForm.Description: Properties are macroscopic observables or order parameters.

DetectionTests: Property vanishes when system is decomposed
Different micro-configurations yield the same property value
Property governs effective behavior at higher levels
FailureModes: Attributing properties to individual elements
Confusing properties with effects or outcomes

TypeRoles.Name: Effect
TypeRoles.Definition: Emergent phenomena understood as fixed outcomes or influences produced by system dynamics that propagate beyond the originating structure. An effect is defined by its impact rather than its internal organization.
FormalCriteria: Existence as an output or consequence of system operation
Ability to influence other systems or levels
Fixation as an observable result

MathForm.Description: Effects are represented as output functionals or boundary impacts.

DetectionTests: Effect persists after internal dynamics are abstracted away
Effect can be measured without reconstructing full system
Effect participates in new causal chains as an input
FailureModes: Confusing effects with properties
Attributing causal structure to pure outcomes

LevelPhases.Name: Differentiation
LevelPhases.Definition: Formation of the basic element set with distinct properties required for subsequent organization.
LevelPhases.Description: This phase marks the emergence of the minimal building blocks that possess the necessary properties to participate in higher-level organization. These elements arise through foundational interactions, typically characterized by simple structures, discrete identities, and inherent dispositions. At this stage, the system does not yet exhibit complex behavior but sets the preconditions for future integration.
LevelPhases.RepeatedSigns: Emergence of distinguishable components, stabilization of basic identities, dominance of local interactions, lack of systemic coordination.
LevelPhases.PredominantInteractionModes: Dispositional causality, local triggering, intrinsic parameter formation, emergence through minimal interaction contexts.

LevelPhases.Name: Integration
LevelPhases.Definition: Cooperative combination of differentiated elements into stable patterns or structural units.
LevelPhases.Description: This phase marks the onset of cooperative interactions between the previously differentiated elements. These interactions give rise to structured assemblies, regular configurations, or dynamic couplings. The emerging units exhibit partial coherence and mutual dependence, often forming the precursors of systemic properties. Patterns of interaction become reproducible, and local coordination mechanisms begin to appear, although full autonomy or regulatory closure is not yet achieved.
LevelPhases.RepeatedSigns: Formation of composite units, emergence of stable interaction patterns, mutual reinforcement or constraint, partial system coherence.
LevelPhases.PredominantInteractionModes: Cooperative dynamics, synergy, structural triggers, semantic alignment, mutual stabilization.

LevelPhases.Name: Self-organization
LevelPhases.Definition: Emergence of regulatory structures that stabilize and coordinate internal dynamics within the system.
LevelPhases.Description: This phase is characterized by the development of internal regulatory mechanisms that coordinate interactions among integrated components. Feedback loops, conditional responses, and adaptive regulation begin to control resource flows, transformations, or signal propagation. The system exhibits increasing autonomy and robustness, maintaining coherence under perturbation. Local rules give rise to global order, and emergent functions begin to appear, though they are still bounded by the constraints of the current level.
LevelPhases.RepeatedSigns: Appearance of feedback loops, dynamic equilibrium, adaptive coordination, resilience to perturbation, emergence of internal differentiation of roles.
LevelPhases.PredominantInteractionModes: Systemic dynamics, feedback control, lateral loops, structural constraints, regulated flows.

LevelPhases.Name: Threshold transition
LevelPhases.Definition: A critical transformation in which the system acquires new properties that cannot be reduced to previous configurations, initiating a new level of organization.
LevelPhases.Description: This phase represents a systemic discontinuity — the culmination of internal organization leads to a qualitative leap. The system begins to exhibit behavior or structural integrity that cannot be fully explained by the dynamics of the previous level. This emergent shift often brings forth novel causal regimes, boundary conditions, or coordination logics. It marks the closure of one cycle and the necessity of describing the system at a higher level of abstraction.
LevelPhases.RepeatedSigns: Appearance of new causality, systemic discontinuity, loss of reducibility, new coherence domain, triggering cross-level effects.
LevelPhases.PredominantInteractionModes: Cross-scale influence, structural triggers, emergent constraints, semantic phase shift, transition to a new organizational logic.

DependencyTypes.Name: Substrate
DependencyTypes.Definition: Dependency type denoting a constitutive material or structural basis required for the phenomenon to be instantiable at all. The parent provides the physical/chemical carrier, building blocks, or persistent structural platform on top of which the child phenomenon can occur, without specifying the detailed control logic of the child.
FormalCriteria: Removal of the parent eliminates the possibility of the phenomenon, not merely its efficiency.
The parent contributes persistent material/structural prerequisites rather than transient modulation.
Functional substitution is possible by an equivalent substrate class, indicating role-level (not identity-level) dependence.
The dependency is primarily about availability/constitution, not about boundary filtering or control.
DetectionTests: Ablation of the parent removes the phenomenon even when other conditions are held constant.
Reintroduction of the substrate restores the phenomenon without requiring a specific regulatory redesign.
The phenomenon’s description references constitutive carriers (molecules, scaffolds, phases) more than control operators.

PhaseRoles.A: Defines the minimal material basis for differentiated components (proto-carriers and building blocks).
PhaseRoles.B: Stabilizes integrated assemblies by providing persistent carriers for coupling.
PhaseRoles.C: Supports sustained self-organization by maintaining the substrate pool and structural persistence.
PhaseRoles.D: Acts as a limiting basis that can saturate and enforce threshold constraints (availability ceiling).

ActivationConditions: The parent is explicitly required as a carrier/building block class.
Dependence is describable without referencing explicit control loops.
Substitution tests or equivalence-class reasoning applies (different substrate, same role).
FailureModes: Confusing resource throughput with constitutive substrate (resource vs substrate).
Treating a boundary condition as substrate.
Over-attributing substrate when the real dependence is catalytic or regulatory.

WeightGuidelines.ConfidenceGuidance: High confidence when ablation/substitution evidence exists; moderate confidence when inferred from constitutive chemistry/physics without direct intervention tests.

DependencyTypes.NotesTemplate: Provides the constitutive substrate/carrier required for the phenomenon to instantiate.

DependencyTypes.Name: Boundary
DependencyTypes.Definition: Dependency type denoting delimitation and interface conditions that separate, filter, or gate interactions between an inside and an outside (or between compartments). The parent supplies boundary constraints (selective permeability, surface/interface rules, compartment walls) that shape admissible flows and stabilize regimes.
FormalCriteria: The dependency primarily operates by restricting/conditioning exchange with an external domain.
The child phenomenon requires a defined inside/outside distinction or an interface geometry.
Boundary properties enforce admissible fluxes (what can enter/leave, at what rate, under which conditions).
The child’s stability depends on boundary maintenance, not only on internal dynamics.
DetectionTests: Changing boundary permeability/selectivity predictably alters the phenomenon’s regime.
Removing the boundary collapses gradients, localization, or persistence of the phenomenon.
Boundary perturbations act faster than internal reconfiguration, indicating boundary-dominated control.

PhaseRoles.A: Enables differentiation by providing early separation and interface constraints.
PhaseRoles.B: Supports integration by stabilizing module interfaces and exchange channels.
PhaseRoles.C: Maintains self-organization by sustaining non-equilibrium conditions and protecting internal structure.
PhaseRoles.D: Becomes a critical control surface where threshold transitions occur via permeability/instability changes.

ActivationConditions: The child phenomenon explicitly depends on selective exchange or containment.
Interface geometry and permeability are mentioned as limiting conditions.
Boundary perturbation experiments are interpretable as causal probes.
FailureModes: Confusing boundary (interface rules) with compartment (internal confinement as a whole).
Overusing boundary when the dependence is actually a generic constraint not tied to interfaces.
Mistaking environment forcing for boundary control.

WeightGuidelines.ConfidenceGuidance: High confidence when permeability/interface manipulation shows regime shifts; moderate confidence when inferred from compartment necessity without direct boundary characterization.

DependencyTypes.NotesTemplate: Imposes boundary/interface conditions (filtering, gating, containment) required for the phenomenon’s regime.

DependencyTypes.Name: Template
DependencyTypes.Definition: Dependency type denoting a guiding structure that biases assembly, copying, or pattern formation by providing a reference configuration. The parent supplies a matching surface, sequence, scaffold, or complementary pattern that constrains admissible outcomes and reduces combinatorial ambiguity during formation.
FormalCriteria: The dependency operates by providing a reference pattern that guides formation (matching, complementarity, alignment).
The child outcome depends on the parent’s configuration (shape/sequence/state) rather than merely its presence.
The parent increases fidelity or selectivity of formation relative to non-templated processes.
Template influence is distinct from catalytic rate enhancement (guidance vs barrier lowering).
DetectionTests: Changing the template configuration changes the produced pattern in a predictable way.
Removal of the template collapses selectivity/fidelity even if substrates remain available.
Template-dependent copying/assembly is detectable as conditional correlation between parent pattern and child outcome.

PhaseRoles.A: Establishes early selective pairing and directed assembly for informational polymers and structured aggregates.
PhaseRoles.B: Supports integration by aligning modules and stabilizing repeatable assembly pathways.
PhaseRoles.C: Constrains self-organization toward reproducible patterns and reduces drift in pattern space.
PhaseRoles.D: Facilitates threshold transitions by enabling persistence and propagation of structured configurations.

ActivationConditions: The child phenomenon implies copying, directed assembly, or pattern inheritance.
Dependence is configuration-sensitive (not just concentration-sensitive).
Fidelity/selectivity metrics are meaningful for validation.
FailureModes: Confusing template (guidance) with information (encoded constraint abstracted from a specific template).
Confusing template with catalyst when only rates change but pattern mapping does not.
Assigning template when the dependence is a generic boundary constraint.

WeightGuidelines.ConfidenceGuidance: High confidence when conditional pattern mapping is measured; moderate confidence when templating is inferred from complementarity without direct fidelity assays.

DependencyTypes.NotesTemplate: Provides a reference configuration that guides/selects admissible assembly or copying outcomes.

DependencyTypes.Name: Catalyst
DependencyTypes.Definition: Dependency type denoting catalytic agency that increases reaction feasibility by lowering activation barriers, increasing rates, or channeling pathways through specific transition states. The parent supplies catalytic structure or active-site-like functionality (enzymes, ribozymes, mineral catalysts) enabling the child process at practical timescales.
FormalCriteria: The dependency primarily changes kinetics (rates, barriers) rather than only boundary conditions.
Catalytic effect can be present at sub-stoichiometric levels relative to substrates.
The parent is not consumed in proportion to product formation (or is regenerated in a cycle).
Specificity can be present as pathway channeling (selective catalysis).
DetectionTests: Rate enhancement is observed upon addition of the parent while keeping substrates constant.
Inhibition or denaturation of the parent reduces reaction throughput and/or alters pathway distribution.
Catalytic signatures exist (turnover, saturation kinetics, activation energy shift).

PhaseRoles.A: Enables feasible synthesis/assembly under otherwise prohibitive kinetic constraints.
PhaseRoles.B: Supports integration by stabilizing throughput and coupling among reaction modules.
PhaseRoles.C: Sustains self-organization by maintaining catalytic closure, selectivity, and network persistence.
PhaseRoles.D: Contributes to threshold transitions when catalytic capacity crosses a saturation/closure boundary.

ActivationConditions: The child phenomenon is rate-limited or barrier-limited without the parent.
Catalytic turnover or barrier-lowering reasoning is applicable.
Intervention (addition/removal/inhibition) is interpretable as causal test.
FailureModes: Mislabeling a structural scaffold as catalyst when no kinetic effect is shown.
Confusing catalyst with template (guidance vs barrier lowering).
Attributing catalysis where the actual dependence is regulation (control) rather than kinetics.

WeightGuidelines.ConfidenceGuidance: High confidence with kinetic assays/inhibition data; moderate confidence with plausible catalytic motifs but limited measurements.

DependencyTypes.NotesTemplate: Provides catalytic functionality enabling feasible kinetics and/or pathway channeling for the phenomenon.

DependencyTypes.Name: Regulation
DependencyTypes.Definition: Dependency type denoting control of rates, states, and regime selection via modulatory mechanisms (switching, gating, homeostatic control, parameter tuning). The parent does not merely supply material; it governs when, where, and how strongly processes operate, often implementing feedback or constraint-based control.
FormalCriteria: The dependency acts on control parameters (gain, thresholds, switching conditions), not only on substrate supply.
The child phenomenon exhibits regime changes contingent on the parent’s control state.
The relation often involves feedback, gating, inhibition, activation, or set-point stabilization.
Regulatory influence can be context-dependent and non-linear.
DetectionTests: Perturbation of the parent changes operating regime (on/off, high/low throughput, alternate pathway selection).
Time-series show control signatures (delays, overshoot, stabilization, hysteresis).
The child phenomenon fails under parameter drift unless the parent control is present.

PhaseRoles.A: Emerges as primitive gating/compatibility constraints that bias early assembly.
PhaseRoles.B: Coordinates integrated modules by aligning rates and preventing destructive interference.
PhaseRoles.C: Maintains stable self-organization via feedback, homeostasis, and adaptive tuning.
PhaseRoles.D: Triggers or suppresses threshold transitions by pushing control parameters across critical boundaries.

ActivationConditions: The child phenomenon depends on controlled balance (rates, concentrations, set-points).
Control loops, gating, inhibition/activation logic is describable.
Evidence can be framed as modulation rather than material provision.
FailureModes: Confusing regulation with static constraint (constraint is fixed boundary; regulation is active or state-dependent control).
Confusing regulation with selection (population filtering vs within-system control).
Overfitting: labeling any influence as regulation without demonstrating regime control.

WeightGuidelines.ConfidenceGuidance: High confidence when modulation experiments show regime control; moderate confidence when inferred from stability requirements without control-loop characterization.

DependencyTypes.NotesTemplate: Modulates control parameters (gating, inhibition/activation, set-points) that determine the phenomenon’s operating regime.

DependencyTypes.Name: Gradient
DependencyTypes.Definition: Dependency type denoting dependence on sustained differences in potential across space, phases, or compartments (concentration, electrochemical, redox, temperature gradients). The parent supplies a non-equilibrium driving condition that biases directionality, coupling efficiency, or stability of processes.
FormalCriteria: The child phenomenon requires a maintained potential difference (not just absolute amounts).
The gradient acts as a driving force, bias, or coupling medium for otherwise unfavorable steps.
Gradient persistence depends on boundary/transport conditions (even if represented elsewhere).
The dependency is directional (uphill/downhill in potential space) rather than purely structural.
DetectionTests: Collapsing the gradient removes or reverses the phenomenon’s bias/coupling.
Artificially establishing the gradient induces the phenomenon or strengthens its efficiency.
Measurements show correlation between gradient magnitude and effect size.

PhaseRoles.A: Appears as primitive energetic biasing in localized environments.
PhaseRoles.B: Supports integrated coupling between reaction modules through sustained driving conditions.
PhaseRoles.C: Maintains self-organization via non-equilibrium stabilization and directional flux shaping.
PhaseRoles.D: Acts as a critical control factor for threshold transitions when gradient magnitude crosses a coupling threshold.

ActivationConditions: The child phenomenon is explicitly non-equilibrium and directional.
Gradient magnitude is measurable or inferable as control variable.
Collapse/restore interventions are feasible conceptually or experimentally.
FailureModes: Confusing gradient with resource (resource is consumable throughput; gradient is a maintained differential).
Confusing gradient with transport (transport creates/uses gradients, but is not the gradient itself).
Treating transient fluctuations as gradients without persistence criteria.

WeightGuidelines.ConfidenceGuidance: High confidence when gradient manipulation changes outcomes; moderate confidence when gradient dependence is inferred from thermodynamic coupling models.

DependencyTypes.NotesTemplate: Provides/maintains a potential difference (electrochemical/redox/concentration) that biases coupling or directionality for the phenomenon.

DependencyTypes.Name: Compartment
DependencyTypes.Definition: Dependency type denoting confinement and localization into a bounded volume or domain that changes effective concentrations, stochasticity, coupling, and protection from dilution. The parent provides compartmental organization (vesicles, droplets, organelles, microdomains) as a context for the child phenomenon.
FormalCriteria: The phenomenon depends on confinement/localization rather than only on global concentrations.
Compartmentalization produces measurable changes in reaction rates, stability, or coupling via concentration and segregation effects.
The dependency is about domain structure (where processes occur), not only about interface selectivity.
The child phenomenon’s persistence is enhanced by reduced mixing/dilution.
DetectionTests: Removing confinement reduces efficiency/persistence due to dilution or uncontrolled mixing.
Introducing compartments increases local throughput or stabilizes networks relative to bulk conditions.
Spatial localization patterns correlate with the phenomenon’s activity.

PhaseRoles.A: Begins as localized microdomains supporting early differentiated assemblies.
PhaseRoles.B: Enables integration by co-localizing complementary modules and reducing interference.
PhaseRoles.C: Stabilizes self-organization through retention, concentration, and protected coupling.
PhaseRoles.D: Becomes central for threshold transitions (proto-cellular closure and replicative persistence).

ActivationConditions: The child phenomenon involves localized networks, retention, or protection from bulk dilution.
A domain-based description (inside/outside, microenvironment) is essential for explanation.
Confinement-dependent rate or stability effects are plausible.
FailureModes: Confusing compartment with boundary (compartment is the domain; boundary is the interface rule).
Assigning compartment when the actual dependence is purely chemical constraint without localization.
Overusing compartment for any spatial heterogeneity without confinement role.

WeightGuidelines.ConfidenceGuidance: High confidence when compartment/no-compartment contrasts exist; moderate confidence when inferred from known confinement benefits without direct comparison.

DependencyTypes.NotesTemplate: Provides confinement/localization that enables concentration, segregation, retention, or protection necessary for the phenomenon.

DependencyTypes.Name: Information
DependencyTypes.Definition: Dependency type denoting reliance on encoded state or pattern (sequence, symbol, code, memory) that constrains or selects system behavior. The parent provides informational content that functions as a causal constraint on the child phenomenon, enabling state-dependent outcomes beyond purely physico-chemical availability.
FormalCriteria: The dependency is configuration-dependent in an informational sense (state carries a mapping to outcomes).
The parent’s informational differences lead to systematically different child outcomes under comparable contexts.
The dependency is abstractable as a code/pattern variable influencing dynamics (not solely a physical template).
Information dependence supports reproducibility across different substrates that realize the same code.
DetectionTests: Changing encoded state changes the child phenomenon predictably while keeping bulk chemistry similar.
Information-preserving transformations maintain function; information-destroying perturbations break it.
Compression/abstraction of the parent to a symbolic/state variable remains predictive for the child.

PhaseRoles.A: Emerges as early state-dependent constraints that bias assembly and persistence.
PhaseRoles.B: Supports integration by coordinating modules through shared encoded states (proto-control codes).
PhaseRoles.C: Stabilizes self-organization by providing memory and state-dependent regulation of pathways.
PhaseRoles.D: Enables threshold transitions when informational closure couples persistence, replication, and compartment dynamics.

ActivationConditions: Outcome variability is explained by state/sequence differences rather than concentration alone.
The parent can be treated as a pattern-carrying variable in explanations and models.
Interventions exist that change informational content without wholesale removal of the carrier.
FailureModes: Confusing information with template (template is a specific guiding structure; information is the abstract encoded constraint).
Attributing information where only boundary or chemical constraints are present.
Treating correlation as information causation without demonstrating state-dependent mapping.

WeightGuidelines.ConfidenceGuidance: High confidence when state-dependent mapping is demonstrated; moderate confidence when inferred from known code-function relations without direct tests in the represented system.

DependencyTypes.NotesTemplate: Provides encoded state/pattern that constrains behavior and enables state-dependent outcomes for the phenomenon.

DependencyTypes.Name: Selection
DependencyTypes.Definition: Dependency type denoting population-level or ensemble-level filtering where differential persistence, reproduction-like amplification, or stability leads to systematic frequency shifts of configurations. The parent supplies selection pressure or filtering dynamics that reshape the distribution of variants relevant to the child phenomenon.
FormalCriteria: The dependency operates on a set of variants with differential persistence/amplification.
Outcome is defined as distributional change over an ensemble, not merely within one instance.
A mapping exists from variant properties to persistence/replication metrics under given conditions.
Selection effect can be separated from mere regulation by requiring population/ensemble semantics.
DetectionTests: Repeated cycles produce consistent frequency shifts correlated with performance/persistence metrics.
Removing the filtering mechanism removes systematic distributional trends (only drift remains).
Selection gradients are measurable as differential survival/amplification rates.

PhaseRoles.A: Typically absent or weak; may appear as differential stability of primitive assemblies.
PhaseRoles.B: Begins as filtering of integrated modules by robustness and compatibility.
PhaseRoles.C: Acts as stabilizing feedback on self-organization by preferential persistence of viable regimes.
PhaseRoles.D: Becomes prominent at threshold transitions where replication-like cycles and compartment persistence generate ensemble-level filtering.

ActivationConditions: An ensemble/population of variants exists (explicitly or implicitly).
A persistence/amplification mechanism exists (growth-division, replication, retention advantage).
Outcome is measurable as frequency shift, not only as individual performance change.
FailureModes: Confusing selection with regulation (within-instance control vs ensemble filtering).
Assigning selection without demonstrating variant ensemble and differential persistence.
Overusing selection where a single-system constraint explains the behavior.

WeightGuidelines.ConfidenceGuidance: High confidence when frequency shifts and differential rates are measured; moderate confidence when only conceptual selection arguments exist.

DependencyTypes.NotesTemplate: Imposes ensemble-level filtering via differential persistence/amplification, shaping the distribution of variants relevant to the phenomenon.

DependencyTypes.Name: Resource
DependencyTypes.Definition: Dependency type denoting reliance on consumable throughput of matter or energy that fuels, feeds, or sustains the phenomenon. The parent provides availability, supply rate, or replenishment of required inputs; unlike substrate, resources are primarily about flow and depletion rather than constitutive presence.
FormalCriteria: The dependency is defined by consumable inputs and supply rates (flux), not only by constitutive carriers.
The child phenomenon’s magnitude/scaling follows resource availability (dose-response or throughput dependence).
Depletion dynamics are relevant: resource exhaustion reduces or halts the phenomenon.
Resource supply can be external or internal, but is measurable as input flow.
DetectionTests: Resource limitation produces predictable decline or regime change in the phenomenon.
Supplementation restores throughput without requiring structural redesign.
Mass/energy balance reveals explicit dependency on input flux.

PhaseRoles.A: Enables early differentiation by making formation steps feasible under supply constraints.
PhaseRoles.B: Supports integration by providing stable throughput required for coupled module operation.
PhaseRoles.C: Sustains self-organization by maintaining non-equilibrium fluxes and reducing collapse from depletion.
PhaseRoles.D: Contributes to threshold transitions when resource throughput crosses a critical sustainment boundary.

ActivationConditions: The phenomenon depends on input throughput and exhibits depletion sensitivity.
Supply limitation/supplementation is a meaningful intervention.
The role is about fueling/feeding rather than constitutive identity.
FailureModes: Confusing resource with substrate (constitutive carrier vs consumable flow).
Using resource for static boundary constraints.
Attributing resource when the key factor is catalytic efficiency or regulation.

WeightGuidelines.ConfidenceGuidance: High confidence with balance/limitation data; moderate confidence when inferred from known energetic/material requirements without direct throughput measurements.

DependencyTypes.NotesTemplate: Provides consumable matter/energy throughput required to sustain or scale the phenomenon.

DependencyTypes.Name: Constraint
DependencyTypes.Definition: Dependency type denoting limiting conditions that restrict degrees of freedom and define an admissible region of state space for the phenomenon (stoichiometric limits, conservation laws, geometric constraints, saturation, compatibility rules). The parent provides the constraint structure that shapes feasible configurations and dynamics.
FormalCriteria: The dependency is best described as restriction of state space or admissible transitions.
Constraint remains meaningful even without detailed dynamics (it bounds what can happen).
Constraint can be expressed as inequalities, invariants, compatibility rules, or conserved quantities.
The phenomenon’s presence requires operating within constrained regimes; outside them it fails.
DetectionTests: Relaxing the constraint increases degrees of freedom and destabilizes or destroys the phenomenon’s regime.
Tightening the constraint reduces variability and may stabilize a specific regime.
Formal constraint representation predicts failure/success boundaries.

PhaseRoles.A: Constrains admissible differentiated configurations, reducing combinatorial explosion.
PhaseRoles.B: Enforces compatibility and stability conditions during integration.
PhaseRoles.C: Defines attractor basins and stability regions for self-organization.
PhaseRoles.D: Determines critical boundaries where threshold transitions become admissible or mandatory.

ActivationConditions: The child phenomenon is defined by admissibility boundaries (feasible/infeasible) more than by throughput.
Constraint statements can be formulated (rules, invariants, inequalities).
Manipulating constraints predictably changes viability.
FailureModes: Confusing active regulation with static constraint.
Using constraint where a specific boundary/interface is the key mechanism (boundary vs constraint).
Treating generic correlation as a constraint without formal or operational boundary definition.

WeightGuidelines.ConfidenceGuidance: High confidence when constraints are operationalized and validated; moderate confidence when constraints are conceptual but not yet measured.

DependencyTypes.NotesTemplate: Imposes limiting conditions that restrict state space and define viability boundaries for the phenomenon.

DependencyTypes.Name: Transport
DependencyTypes.Definition: Dependency type denoting dependence on movement/exchange processes that deliver inputs, remove outputs, connect compartments, or maintain fluxes (diffusion, convection, channel-mediated flow, active transport). The parent provides transport capacity enabling sustained coupling and spatially distributed dynamics.
FormalCriteria: The dependency is about flux-mediated coupling across space or between domains.
Transport capacity and kinetics are relevant control variables (permeability is boundary; transport is the movement process).
The child phenomenon depends on delivery/removal timescales relative to reaction or organization timescales.
Transport can create, sustain, or dissipate gradients depending on coupling.
DetectionTests: Blocking transport alters the phenomenon even if substrates/resources are present.
Transport rate changes modulate the phenomenon’s stability or throughput.
Spatial patterns/heterogeneities track transport pathways.

PhaseRoles.A: Enables initial delivery and local concentration needed for differentiated assembly.
PhaseRoles.B: Coordinates exchange among integrated modules and reduces bottlenecks.
PhaseRoles.C: Sustains self-organization by stabilizing flux balance and enabling distributed coupling.
PhaseRoles.D: Acts as a critical bottleneck; transport saturation or gating can trigger threshold transitions.

ActivationConditions: The phenomenon is flux-limited or requires exchange coupling between domains.
Transport blocking experiments or reasoning are applicable.
Temporal alignment between transport and internal dynamics is central to explanation.
FailureModes: Confusing transport with gradient (gradient is a maintained differential; transport is the flux mechanism).
Confusing transport with boundary (boundary is interface rule; transport is movement dynamics).
Attributing transport where only static localization (compartment) is relevant.

WeightGuidelines.ConfidenceGuidance: High confidence when flux blocking/modulation produces predicted changes; moderate confidence when transport is inferred but not measured.

DependencyTypes.NotesTemplate: Provides flux/movement processes that deliver/remove/redistribute components required for the phenomenon’s coupling or persistence.

DependencyTypes.Name: Environment
DependencyTypes.Definition: Dependency type denoting reliance on external conditions not internal to the system’s represented organization level (temperature, pressure, solvent composition, radiation, external fields, ecological context, geochemical setting). The parent provides exogenous boundary data that sets feasibility and stability regimes for the phenomenon.
FormalCriteria: The dependency originates outside the represented system boundary (exogenous parameters or fields).
Environmental variables define feasibility regions or shift regime boundaries for the child phenomenon.
The phenomenon’s stability is contingent on maintaining external ranges (not only internal regulation).
Environmental influence can be quasi-static or fluctuating; in both cases it is exogenous.
DetectionTests: External parameter sweeps (temperature, pH of bulk, ionic strength, field strength) produce predictable regime shifts.
Holding internal composition constant while changing external conditions changes the phenomenon.
The phenomenon’s description includes environmental ranges or setting-specific admissibility conditions.

PhaseRoles.A: Defines admissibility windows for initial differentiation and assembly feasibility.
PhaseRoles.B: Sets stable operating envelopes for integrated modules and suppresses destructive regimes.
PhaseRoles.C: Constrains long-term self-organization by controlling external dissipation channels and boundary conditions.
PhaseRoles.D: Controls critical thresholds by shifting parameter landscapes and enabling/disabling transition pathways.

ActivationConditions: The child phenomenon requires a specific setting or parameter range external to the system boundary.
Environmental perturbations are interpretable as exogenous forcing.
The dependence cannot be reduced to internal substrate/catalyst/regulation alone.
FailureModes: Using environment when the correct label is boundary (interface) or resource (throughput).
Treating internally maintained set-points as environment (confusing internal regulation with exogenous conditions).
Overgeneralization: environment used as a catch-all without specifying operational variables.

WeightGuidelines.ConfidenceGuidance: High confidence when regime maps vs external variables exist; moderate confidence when dependence is inferred from plausibility arguments without parameter sweeps.

DependencyTypes.NotesTemplate: Provides exogenous boundary conditions (setting variables/fields) that define feasibility and stability regimes for the phenomenon.

CarrierGroups.Name: Field / mode carriers
CarrierGroups.Definition: Carrier group in which the countable units are field excitations, coherent modes, or resonant components whose coupling constitutes the causal carrier of an edge.

CountingOntology.WhatIsCounted: Distinct excitation or mode instances admitted by the chosen field/mode representation and participating in the same interaction motif.
CountingOntology.UnitBoundaryRule: Mode identity is defined by representation-specific labels (mode index, band, resonance label) and coherence/persistence within the motif window.
CountingOntology.MembershipRule: A unit is counted only if it participates in the same coupling motif relevant to the edge’s mechanism (not merely co-present in the system).

QuantitativeNotes: Typical structural thresholds arise from minimal closure motifs in mode-coupling space.
\(N_{crit}\) is frequently small (often 3 for triadic closure) and defined by admissibility constraints rather than bulk statistics.
\(N_{sat}\) is usually model-dependent and may be omitted when additional modes only scale amplitude without changing regime.
FailureModes: Counting energy quanta or intensity samples instead of mode instances (wrong unit kind).
Counting modes that are not coherently participating in the coupling motif (wrong membership).
Using FM carriers where object-body individuation is the valid description (wrong carrier ontology).

CarrierGroups.Name: Physical body carriers
CarrierGroups.Definition: Carrier group in which the countable units are discrete bodies or object-like participants whose mutual interactions constitute the causal carrier of an edge.

CountingOntology.WhatIsCounted: Individuated bodies treated as separable participants in a shared interaction motif (N-body configuration).
CountingOntology.UnitBoundaryRule: Body boundary is defined at the chosen descriptive scale by operational separability and persistence across the motif window.
CountingOntology.MembershipRule: A body is counted only if it is dynamically coupled into the motif relevant to the edge (not merely nearby or present).

QuantitativeNotes: \(N_{min}\) is typically 2 for interaction-defined edges, but may be 1 for edges representing self-constraints on an already-individuated body.
\(N_{crit}\) is often structural/topological (e.g., closure at 3) rather than statistical.
\(N_{sat}\) may be absent; beyond certain \(N\), additional bodies may primarily increase degrees of freedom rather than introduce new qualitative regimes.
FailureModes: Counting parts/subcomponents as bodies (wrong boundary).
Counting bodies not actually coupled into the motif (wrong membership).
Replacing body count with link count without declaring a topological carrier (carrier confusion).

CarrierGroups.Name: Functional material carriers
CarrierGroups.Definition: Carrier group in which the countable units are functionally relevant material units such as molecules, bonds, active sites, complexes, or cells, whose quantities control activation or regime of an edge.

CountingOntology.WhatIsCounted: Functionally typed material units that instantiate the mechanism of the edge (e.g., molecules for intermolecular motifs, bonds for coupling density, active sites for catalysis, complexes for modular function, cells for cellular-scale mechanisms).
CountingOntology.UnitBoundaryRule: Unit boundary is defined by functional individuation at the chosen level (chemical identity, bond instance, active-site identity, complex membership, cell boundary).
CountingOntology.MembershipRule: A unit is counted only if it participates in the mechanism-relevant motif for the edge (e.g., bond instances contributing to a percolating network; active sites participating in throughput).

QuantitativeNotes: Two quantitative regimes commonly coexist: small-N cooperative/stoichiometric thresholds and large-N collective/phase thresholds.
\(N_{crit}\) is frequently context-dependent (temperature, boundary, concentration, geometry) for collective-phase behavior.
\(N_{sat}\) is meaningful when increasing unit count stops changing regime and only scales throughput or amplitude.
FailureModes: Counting bulk amount or concentration without a unit mapping (unit mismatch).
Mixing different unit kinds in a single edge without declaring a composite carrier rule (type mixing).
Using functional material units to represent purely structural graph thresholds (should be topological carriers).

CarrierGroups.Name: Topological / configurational carriers
CarrierGroups.Definition: Carrier group in which the countable units are structural elements of the causal configuration (edges, cycles, cores, components) whose abundance or presence controls activation or regime of an edge.

CountingOntology.WhatIsCounted: Instances of specified structural elements in the typed causal graph (edge instances, simple cycles, feedback loops, strongly connected components, k-core membership, connected components, percolating component).
CountingOntology.UnitBoundaryRule: Element identity is defined by graph-theoretic criteria under the ontology’s typing constraints (interaction modes, causal directions, role constraints).
CountingOntology.MembershipRule: Only elements that satisfy the motif specification for the edge are counted (e.g., only cycles containing specific interaction modes; only SCCs above a size threshold).

QuantitativeNotes: Small-N thresholds (3–7) typically correspond to motif closure (cycles, minimal feedback, minimal cores).
Large-N thresholds often correspond to percolation/connectivity transitions (system-spanning component).
\(N_{crit}\) can be defined as a structural predicate threshold (e.g., existence of at least one qualifying cycle; k-core \(\geq\) \(k_{min}\).
FailureModes: Counting all edges/cycles without enforcing motif typing constraints (overcount).
Confusing percolation in the causal graph with physical percolation without declaring mapping (category error).
Using topological carriers when the edge’s mechanism is explicitly material/functional (wrong ontology).

CarrierGroups.Name: Informational / symbolic carriers
CarrierGroups.Definition: Carrier group in which the countable units are informational or symbolic units (tokens, symbols, memory states, effective parameters) whose quantity controls activation or regime of an edge.

CountingOntology.WhatIsCounted: Discrete informational units defined by the representation (token occurrences, symbol instances, distinct states, parameter instances) that participate in the causal mechanism of the edge.
CountingOntology.UnitBoundaryRule: Unit identity is defined by representation-level distinctness (token type, symbol identity, state label, parameter identity) within the motif window.
CountingOntology.MembershipRule: Only units that causally participate in the mechanism are counted (e.g., tokens that enter a propagation loop; states that are reachable under the constraint).

QuantitativeNotes: Thresholds can reflect minimal expressivity (minimal alphabet/length), minimal memory for control, or distributional stabilization in large ensembles.
\(N_{crit}\) may represent a minimal codebook size, minimal state-space coverage, or minimal parameter set for regime selection.
\(N_{sat}\) may reflect diminishing structural novelty beyond a representation capacity threshold.
FailureModes: Counting raw data volume without defining symbolic units (unit mismatch).
Mixing token count with agent count without declaring the carrier ontology (type mixing).
Treating informational carriers as purely topological without representation semantics (loss of meaning).

CarrierGroups.Name: Agent / population carriers
CarrierGroups.Definition: Carrier group in which the countable units are agents, population members, role instances, or interaction events whose quantity controls activation or regime of an edge at social or collective scales.

CountingOntology.WhatIsCounted: Agents (autonomous decision-capable participants), population members, instantiated roles, or interaction events, depending on the specified carrier subtype for the edge.
CountingOntology.UnitBoundaryRule: Unit identity is defined by participation identity at the relevant scale (agent identity, role-instance identity, event identity) within the motif window.
CountingOntology.MembershipRule: Only units participating in the relevant collective mechanism are counted (e.g., members inside the same coordination regime; events inside the same cascade).

QuantitativeNotes: Thresholds often correspond to regime stabilization in coordination, norm formation, market formation, or institutional persistence.
\(N_{crit}\) is commonly context-dependent and may be defined via structural predicates (e.g., sustained participation, stability over time) rather than a single universal number.
\(N_{sat}\) may represent saturation of participation where further growth changes scale more than causal regime.
FailureModes: Counting passive exposure as participation (wrong membership).
Counting agents without specifying the mechanism (role vs event vs member ambiguity).
Projecting micro-level physical carriers into agent-level edges without an explicit mapping (category error).

CarrierTypes.Name: Physical body
CarrierTypes.Definition: Discrete material body treated as an individuated participant in an interaction configuration.
CarrierTypes.IndividuationRule: Geometric or operational separability with persistence over the motif window.
CarrierTypes.MembershipRule: Counted only if dynamically coupled within the specific causal motif.

CarrierTypes.Name: Field excitation
CarrierTypes.Definition: Localized excitation instance within a field acting as a countable interaction participant.
CarrierTypes.IndividuationRule: Distinguished by mode index, localization, or coherence in representation space.
CarrierTypes.MembershipRule: Counted only if participating in the same coupling motif.

CarrierTypes.Name: Resonant mode
CarrierTypes.Definition: Coherently sustained oscillatory mode functioning as a causal carrier.
CarrierTypes.IndividuationRule: Stable resonance parameters under admissibility constraints.
CarrierTypes.MembershipRule: Included only if coherence is maintained within the motif.

CarrierTypes.Name: Atom
CarrierTypes.Definition: Individual atom acting as a discrete participant in bonding or structural interaction.
CarrierTypes.IndividuationRule: Chemical identity at the selected scale.
CarrierTypes.MembershipRule: Counted only if participating in the bonding motif.

CarrierTypes.Name: Molecule
CarrierTypes.Definition: Discrete molecule functioning as a causal unit in interactions or assemblies.
CarrierTypes.IndividuationRule: Stable molecular identity under given conditions.
CarrierTypes.MembershipRule: Counted only if participating in the relevant motif.

CarrierTypes.Name: Bond instance
CarrierTypes.Definition: Single instantiated chemical bond contributing to structural coupling.
CarrierTypes.IndividuationRule: Defined by bonded pair and bond type.
CarrierTypes.MembershipRule: Counted only if contributing to the structural motif.

CarrierTypes.Name: Active site
CarrierTypes.Definition: Localized catalytic or functional region enabling selective interaction.
CarrierTypes.MembershipRule: Counted only if participating in catalytic throughput.

CarrierTypes.Name: Macromolecular complex
CarrierTypes.Definition: Discrete multi-molecule assembly functioning as a unified causal unit.
CarrierTypes.MembershipRule: Counted if functioning as an integrated module.

CarrierTypes.Name: Cell
CarrierTypes.Definition: Autonomous living unit functioning as a causal participant.
CarrierTypes.MembershipRule: Counted if participating in the same physiological motif.

CarrierTypes.Name: Interaction edge
CarrierTypes.Definition: Single instantiated causal link within a configuration.
CarrierTypes.IndividuationRule: Ordered node pair with typed interaction.
CarrierTypes.MembershipRule: Counted only if matching motif constraints.

CarrierTypes.Name: Feedback loop
CarrierTypes.Definition: Closed causal loop acting as a regulatory structural carrier.
CarrierTypes.MembershipRule: Counted if structurally closed and active.

CarrierTypes.Name: Information token
CarrierTypes.Definition: Discrete symbolic or informational unit participating in propagation or regulation.
CarrierTypes.MembershipRule: Counted if participating in the same informational motif.

CarrierTypes.Name: Agent
CarrierTypes.Definition: Autonomous decision-capable participant in a collective configuration.
CarrierTypes.IndividuationRule: Participation identity at the selected scale.
CarrierTypes.MembershipRule: Counted if participating in the same coordination regime.

CarrierTypes.Name: Neural state / activity pattern
CarrierTypes.Definition: Discrete neurodynamic state or recurrent activity pattern participating in causal modulation, integration, or control across neural and social-neural interfaces.
CarrierTypes.CountingUnit: One distinguishable neural state or activity pattern.
CarrierTypes.IndividuationRule: A unit is individuated by state-pattern identity at the selected resolution, defined by a stable configuration of neural activation, synchronization, or dynamical regime that is causally distinguishable within the mechanism.
CarrierTypes.MembershipRule: Counted if participating in the same neurodynamic motif, regulatory loop, or socially coupled neural mechanism relevant to the edge.

ComplexityLevels.Name: Proto Field
ComplexityLevels.Innovation: Hypothesized substrates preceding known physics; admits non-standard metrics and pre-causal regularities that could seed later physical law.
ComplexityLevels.NewSelfOrganizing: Organization, if present, is not constrained by standard spacetime; putative structures act as sources of randomness or hidden boundary data for lower levels.
ComplexityLevels.KeyAspects: Methodological limit; placeholder for theories beyond empirical reach; supports meta-constraints shaping quantum vacuum and constants.

ComplexityLevels.Name: Quantum
ComplexityLevels.Innovation: Elementary fields and symmetries define conserved charges and interaction vertices; vacuum fluctuations and entanglement set nonlocal constraints.
ComplexityLevels.NewSelfOrganizing: Color confinement forms hadrons; symmetry breaking and running couplings specify effective low-energy behavior.
ComplexityLevels.KeyAspects: Field quanta and constants; renormalization logic; foundations for atomic binding and all higher organization.

ComplexityLevels.Name: Atomic
ComplexityLevels.Innovation: Bound electron states produce stable atoms; bonding taxonomies (covalent, ionic, metallic, van der Waals, hydrogen) generate combinatorial diversity.
ComplexityLevels.NewSelfOrganizing: Energetics and symmetry of orbitals yield periodicity and reaction pathways; supramolecular motifs prepare membranes and templating.
ComplexityLevels.KeyAspects: Electronic structure governs reactivity; phase behavior and lattice formation; substrate for materials and biochemistry.

ComplexityLevels.Name: Biochemical
ComplexityLevels.Innovation: Sequence-coded macromolecules enable replication and catalysis; folding creates functional micro-machines.
ComplexityLevels.NewSelfOrganizing: Autocatalytic sets and feedback-controlled metabolism stabilize production and energy transduction.
ComplexityLevels.KeyAspects: Genetic encoding, kinetic control, compartment-ready chemistries; bridge to cellular autonomy.

ComplexityLevels.Name: Cellular
ComplexityLevels.Innovation: Boundary formation and organelles localize chemistry; regulated transport and signaling maintain viable internal conditions.
ComplexityLevels.NewSelfOrganizing: Feedback circuits govern growth, division, and stress response; spatial organization supports function.
ComplexityLevels.KeyAspects: Autonomy, reproduction, regulated information flow; platform for specialization and tissue formation.

ComplexityLevels.Name: Multicellular
ComplexityLevels.Innovation: Division of labor and morphogenetic programs produce structured tissues and organs with coordinated functions.
ComplexityLevels.NewSelfOrganizing: Mechanical and chemical gradients, plus gene-regulatory networks, orchestrate form and repair.
ComplexityLevels.KeyAspects: Morphogenesis, organogenesis, systemic regulation; resilience via redundancy and regeneration.

ComplexityLevels.Name: Behavioral
ComplexityLevels.Innovation: Distributed circuits transform sensation into adaptive action; plasticity tunes performance to context.
ComplexityLevels.NewSelfOrganizing: Feedback across sensory–motor loops and internal models stabilizes behavior and supports abstraction.
ComplexityLevels.KeyAspects: Representation, prediction, policy selection; conditions for subjective experience in some organisms.

ComplexityLevels.Name: Social
ComplexityLevels.Innovation: Symbolic communication and shared memory enable cumulative knowledge and complex organization.
ComplexityLevels.NewSelfOrganizing: Norms, markets, and governance reduce friction and align incentives across populations.
ComplexityLevels.KeyAspects: Collective intelligence, cooperation–conflict dynamics, cultural evolution; macro-patterns from local rules.

ComplexityLevels.Name: Technological
ComplexityLevels.Innovation: Artificial systems extend sensing, memory, and control; code becomes an agent of design and coordination.
ComplexityLevels.NewSelfOrganizing: Machine-to-machine protocols and data flows create emergent capability and optimization regimes.
ComplexityLevels.KeyAspects: Automation, AI governance, human–machine coupling; acceleration of discovery and deployment.

ComplexityLevels.Name: Planetary
ComplexityLevels.Innovation: Coupled atmosphere–ocean–land–biosphere processes regulate habitability; anthropogenic forcings modify cycles.
ComplexityLevels.NewSelfOrganizing: Ecosystems and networks co-adapt across basins and continents; risk cascades motivate global responses.
ComplexityLevels.KeyAspects: Earth-system resilience, tipping elements, cross-border infrastructures; sustainability constraints.

ComplexityLevels.Name: Civilizational
ComplexityLevels.Innovation: Interlinked energy, materials, and information systems coordinate at continental scale; institutions manage externalities.
ComplexityLevels.NewSelfOrganizing: Standards, protocols, and incentive architectures align actors under uncertainty and shocks.
ComplexityLevels.KeyAspects: Long-horizon planning, circularity, equity constraints; interface to orbital expansion.

ComplexityLevels.Name: Spacefaring infrastructure
ComplexityLevels.Innovation: Orbital and deep-space assets extend sensing, manufacturing, and habitation beyond Earth.
ComplexityLevels.NewSelfOrganizing: Multi-body dynamics, radiation environments, and sparse-resource economics shape robust architectures.
ComplexityLevels.KeyAspects: Launch–in-space value chains, on-orbit servicing, ISRU; foundations for diasporic expansion.

ComplexityLevels.Name: Cosmic
ComplexityLevels.Innovation: Large-scale structure emerges from gravity, dark matter, and dark energy; relics encode formation histories.
ComplexityLevels.NewSelfOrganizing: Resonances, feedback from star formation and AGN, and mergers scaffold long-range order.
ComplexityLevels.KeyAspects: Cosmic web topology, horizon limits, astroengineering possibility space; terminal boundary conditions.

ComplexityLevels.Name: Upper speculative
ComplexityLevels.Innovation: All levels treated as local modalities of a broader structural field; causation and organization become parameters.
ComplexityLevels.NewSelfOrganizing: Transitions operate on types of causality and admissibility rather than on carriers; pattern compatibility spans worlds.
ComplexityLevels.KeyAspects: Methodological closure of ontology; scope conditions for theory transfer and limits of reducibility.

Ontologicals.Name: Quantum Mechanics
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of the behavior of matter and energy at the smallest scales, where particles exhibit wave-particle duality and probabilistic outcomes.

Ontologicals.Name: Physics
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of matter, energy, interactions, and the fundamental laws governing natural phenomena.

Ontologicals.Name: Chemistry
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of the composition, structure, properties, and transformations of matter.

Ontologicals.Name: Biology
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of living organisms, their structures, functions, and interactions.

Ontologicals.Name: Ecology
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of ecosystems and the interactions between organisms and their environments.

Ontologicals.Name: Neuroscience
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of the nervous system, neural activity, and brain function.

Ontologicals.Name: Psychology
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of behavior, cognition, and mental processes.

Ontologicals.Name: Sociology
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of social structures, collective behavior, and institutional dynamics.

Ontologicals.Name: Economics
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of production, distribution, and consumption of goods and services.

Ontologicals.Name: Technology
Ontologicals.Definition: The application of scientific knowledge for the design of tools, systems, and practical solutions.

Ontologicals.Name: Cultural Studies
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of cultural practices, symbolic systems, and shared meaning-making.

Ontologicals.Name: Cognitive Science
Ontologicals.Definition: The interdisciplinary study of perception, memory, reasoning, and intelligence.

Ontologicals.Name: Linguistics
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of language, its structure, function, and evolution.

Ontologicals.Name: Philosophy
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of fundamental questions concerning reality, knowledge, logic, and ethics.

Ontologicals.Name: Mathematics
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of quantitative structures, patterns, formal systems, and abstract relationships.

Ontologicals.Name: History
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of past events, their causes, and their influence on present conditions.

Ontologicals.Name: Art Studies
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of visual, auditory, and performative artistic practices and their formal structures.

Ontologicals.Name: Literary Studies
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of written works, narrative structures, and their cultural roles.

Ontologicals.Name: Musicology
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of music, including its structure, composition, and cultural context.

Ontologicals.Name: Theatre Studies
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of dramatic performance, stagecraft, and theatrical traditions.

Ontologicals.Name: Film Studies
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of cinema, moving images, and audiovisual narrative structures.

Ontologicals.Name: Journalism
Ontologicals.Definition: The study and practice of producing and communicating news and factual information.

Ontologicals.Name: Political Science
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of governance, power structures, and political behavior.

Ontologicals.Name: Law
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of legal systems, regulations, and principles governing human conduct.

Ontologicals.Name: Education
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of teaching, learning processes, and educational systems.

Ontologicals.Name: Medicine
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of health, disease, diagnosis, and medical treatment.

Ontologicals.Name: Computer Science
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of computation, algorithms, information processing, and digital systems.

Ontologicals.Name: Information Science
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of information structures, storage, retrieval, and flow in systems.

Ontologicals.Name: Data Science
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of extracting knowledge and patterns from structured and unstructured data.

Ontologicals.Name: Systems Science
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of complex systems, their organization, interactions, and dynamics.

Ontologicals.Name: Cybernetics
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of control, regulation, communication, and feedback in biological, mechanical, and social systems.

Ontologicals.Name: Complexity Science
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of emergent behavior, nonlinear dynamics, and organization in complex adaptive systems.

Ontologicals.Name: Geology
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of Earth's physical structure, materials, and geodynamic processes.

Ontologicals.Name: Astronomy
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of celestial objects, cosmic structures, and the physical universe beyond Earth.

Ontologicals.Name: Astrophysics
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of physical processes governing stars, galaxies, and cosmological structures.

Ontologicals.Name: Geography
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of spatial distributions, environments, and interactions across the Earth's surface.

Ontologicals.Name: Anthropology
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of human origins, cultures, societies, and biological variation.

Ontologicals.Name: Archaeology
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of past human societies through material remains and artifacts.

Ontologicals.Name: Ethics
Ontologicals.Definition: The systematic study of moral principles, values, and normative frameworks.

Ontologicals.Name: Logic
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of valid reasoning, formal systems, and inferential structures.

Ontologicals.Name: Environmental Science
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of environmental systems, climate dynamics, and anthropogenic impacts.

Ontologicals.Name: Materials Science
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of material properties, structures, and their technological applications.

Ontologicals.Name: Engineering
Ontologicals.Definition: The application of design, analysis, and optimization principles to create functional systems and structures.

Ontologicals.Name: Robotics
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of autonomous machines, actuation, perception, and human–machine interaction.

Ontologicals.Name: Artificial Intelligence
Ontologicals.Definition: The study of models and algorithms enabling machines to learn, reason, and act autonomously.
